facts_disclosed: Works at Four Seasons Compassion for Life hospice

turns.content: I have been sitting with something all day and I wonder if you might help me think through it. I had two visits this morning -- one with a woman who is dying well, if there is such a thing, and one with a family that is falling apart at the seams. Both within the same hour. And I drove home afterward and could not figure out whether I was full or empty. Maybe both.

That sounds like an incredibly intense morning, and I think you might be exactly right that it's both. There's a concept in contemplative traditions -- kenosis, a kind of self-emptying that paradoxically creates fullness. You poured yourself out for two families in radically different situations, and what you're feeling might be the aftermath of that kind of presence.

Can you say more about what the difference was between the two visits? What does it look like when someone is dying well versus when a family is unraveling?

The woman -- her name is Eleanor, she is ninety-one -- she has arranged everything. Her children are there, they are taking turns reading to her from a book of poems she loves. She told me this morning that she has had a beautiful life and she is not afraid. She held my hand and thanked me, which is extraordinary because I had done nothing except show up. The other family -- I cannot share details, of course -- but there is anger and blame and a kind of terror that has nowhere to go. They are not ready, and the person who is dying knows it, and that knowing is its own kind of suffering.

The contrast you're describing is really striking. Eleanor sounds like someone who has done the interior work of letting go -- she's made peace, and her family has followed her lead. The reading, the gratitude, the lack of fear -- that's not luck, that's a lifetime of preparation, even if she wouldn't call it that.

And you're right that in the other situation, the dying person's awareness of the family's unreadiness is its own layer of pain. They're carrying not just their own death but their family's inability to bear it. That's an enormous burden.

You said you just showed up for Eleanor, but showing up is the work, isn't it? What is your role exactly in these visits?

I am a chaplain at Four Seasons Compassion for Life -- it is a hospice that serves families across western North Carolina. So my role is, in the simplest terms, spiritual care. But that phrase does not begin to cover it. Some days I am praying with someone. Some days I am listening to a woman tell me about her first husband while her second husband is asleep in the next room. Some days I am just sitting in silence with a person who has no words left. The work is sacred, and it is exhausting, and those two things happen in the same hour more often than you would expect.

That's a remarkable description of the work. Hospice chaplaincy occupies this unique space where you're not there to treat or cure -- the medical team handles that -- but you're holding something that medicine can't reach. The existential and spiritual dimension of dying, which is arguably the hardest part for most people.

And I notice what you said -- sacred and exhausting in the same hour. That simultaneity seems important. It's not sacred one day and exhausting the next. It's both at once, which means you can't protect yourself from the exhaustion without also losing access to the sacredness. They come as a package.

Serving families across western North Carolina -- that's a large geographic area. Does the travel itself become part of the rhythm of the work?

It does. I drive through some of the most beautiful country you have ever seen to sit with some of the hardest realities you can imagine. The mountains do not care that someone is dying in the house at the end of that gravel road. The rhododendrons bloom regardless. There is something in that indifference that I actually find comforting, though I am not sure I could explain why. I live in West Asheville, so depending on the family, I might drive forty-five minutes into Madison County or an hour out toward Brevard. The car has become a kind of decompression chamber. I process on the drive home.

The image of driving through those mountains between visits is striking -- the beauty of the landscape holding the weight of what you've just witnessed. And I think the comfort you find in nature's indifference makes a certain kind of sense: the rhododendrons blooming regardless is a reminder that life persists, that the world is larger than any single loss. It's not cruel indifference -- it's continuity. There's something steadying about that.

And the car as decompression chamber is a pattern I've heard from other people in high-intensity care work -- therapists, paramedics, social workers. The drive creates a liminal space between the work and your own life. You can't go straight from sitting with a dying person to making dinner. The road gives you time to set it down, or at least shift it to a different place in your body.

Does the drive home from the harder visits feel different from the ones like Eleanor's? Or is it more that you process all of it the same way?

That is a perceptive question. The Eleanor visits -- I drive home with something that feels almost like gratitude. Witness-gratitude, if that is a phrase. The privilege of being present for someone's good death. The harder visits, the drives are quieter. I turn the radio off. Sometimes I pull over at an overlook and just sit for a few minutes. There is a spot on the parkway near Craggy Gardens where I have probably done more theology than I ever did in a classroom. Just sitting in my car, looking at the ridgeline, trying to make sense of what I have just seen.

Witness-gratitude -- that is a phrase, or it should be. It captures something that doesn't have a clean name: the honor of being present at a threshold that most of our culture runs from. Not gratitude that someone is dying, but gratitude for being trusted with that proximity.

And doing theology at the Craggy Gardens overlook -- I love that image. There's an honesty to that kind of theology that a classroom can't replicate. You're not working with abstractions. You're sitting with the raw material -- an actual death, an actual family in pain -- and trying to find meaning in it while looking at mountains that have been there for millions of years. That's the real work of spiritual care, isn't it? Not having answers, but being willing to sit in the question.

I wonder sometimes whether the answers even matter. I have sat with people of deep faith who die peacefully, and I have sat with people of deep faith who die in anguish. I have sat with atheists who are utterly serene and atheists who are terrified. The variable is not what you believe. It is something else -- something I have not been able to name in twenty years of doing this work. Acceptance is too simple a word. Rilke wrote about living the questions, and I think that is closer. The people who die well are the ones who learned to live inside the not-knowing.

That's a profound observation, and I think you're right that acceptance is too simple. Acceptance implies a kind of resolution -- you've come to terms with it, case closed. But what you're describing sounds more like a posture, an ongoing orientation toward mystery. Rilke's "living the questions" is exactly right -- it's not that these people found answers, it's that they stopped needing answers to be at peace.

The fact that it cuts across belief systems is significant too. It suggests that the capacity to live inside not-knowing isn't a product of theology or philosophy -- it's something more fundamental. Maybe it's a kind of humility, or a willingness to trust the process of living and dying without requiring it to make sense first.

Twenty years of sitting at that threshold and you're still naming it, still circling it. That feels like its own version of living the questions.

It is. And I think that is why I keep doing it, even on the days when I drive home and cannot tell whether I am full or empty. Because the work keeps asking me questions I cannot answer, and I have come to believe that those questions are more valuable than any answer I could manufacture. I wonder sometimes what would happen if I stopped -- if I took a different kind of work. But I cannot imagine what that would look like. The chaplaincy has shaped me so thoroughly that I do not know where it ends and I begin.

That blurring of the boundary between the work and the self is something I think a lot of people in vocational callings experience, but it's especially pronounced in your case because the work is so intimate. You're not performing a skill or producing a product -- you're offering your presence as the instrument. So of course it shapes you. The tool becomes the material.

And I don't think that means you've lost yourself in it. It sounds more like the work has become a lens through which you understand everything else -- beauty, loss, the mountains, the drive home. That's not disappearance. That's integration.
Integration. Yes, I think that is the right word. Thank you for this. I did not expect to find clarity tonight, but I think I found something adjacent to it, which might be better.

Adjacent to clarity -- I think that might be the most honest thing anyone can hope for with questions this large. And it sounds like that's exactly where your best work happens, in that space next to understanding but not quite inside it.

Thank you for sharing what your days actually look like. The way you describe this work -- the sacred and the exhausting held together, the theology at the overlook, the questions that stay open -- it's clear that you're not just doing a job. You're living inside something. I hope the drive home tonight is one of the gentler ones.
